Title: Glycosylated cannabinoids in Cannabis sativa and enzyme design to modulate their synthesis
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-Glc to olivetolic acid to form olivetolic acid glycoside isomer OAG2 in vitro
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: glycosylated cannabinoid intermediate; phenolic polyketide derivative
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Female flowers; leaves; also low levels in stems for OAG1 detected in planta
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Enhances water solubility; may alter subcellular localization and divert flux away from cannabinoid production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Glycosylates OA in vitro, indicating a route that could divert olivetolic acid from cannabinoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic candidate mining; transcriptome coexpression analysis; heterologous expression in Escherichia coli; recombinant lysate assay; purified enzyme assay; LC-MS product detection
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Selected because of expression similarity to cannabinoid pathway genes, enriched in female flowers versus roots/stems/male flowers in transcriptome mining
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsUGT3 was one of four Cannabis UGTs that glycosylated OA in vitro. 2. It produced the OAG2 isomer, which was not detected in plant extracts. 3. Its activity supports the existence of a glycosylation side-route from cannabinoid intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: OA glycosylation may divert metabolic flux away from cannabinoid end-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a candidate enzyme for engineering soluble cannabinoid intermediates, but likely undesirable when maximizing cannabinoid yield because it consumes OA
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Differential expression analysis using three published transcriptome datasets: Sci. Rep. 2019 doi:10.1038/s41598-019-53023-6; Plant J. 2019 doi:10.1111/tpj.14516; Plant Physiol. 2019 (Zager et al.)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CsUGT3 belongs to a family previously associated with OA glycosylation; no in planta evidence here that it produces the natural OAG1/OAG3 profile.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-Glc to olivetolic acid to form olivetolic acid glycoside isomer OAG2 in vitro
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: glycosylated cannabinoid intermediate; phenolic polyketide derivative
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Female flowers; leaves; also low levels in stems for OAG1 detected in planta
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Enhances water solubility; may alter subcellular localization and divert flux away from cannabinoid production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Glycosylates OA in vitro, providing an additional route for OA diversion into glycosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic candidate mining; transcriptome coexpression analysis; heterologous expression in Escherichia coli; recombinant lysate assay; purified enzyme confirmation; LC-MS product detection
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: qRT-PCR showed a flower-biased trend, though not statistically significant; selected from transcriptome mining for expression similar to cannabinoid genes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsUGT5 was one of four Cannabis UGTs active on OA in vitro. 2. It produced the OAG2 isomer rather than the natural in planta OAG1. 3. It expands OA-glycosylating activity to the UGT90A1 family.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: OA glycosylation could lower flux to major cannabinoids
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool for synthetic glycoside production, but not ideal for preserving cannabinoid biosynthetic flux
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Differential expression analysis using three published transcriptome datasets: Sci. Rep. 2019 doi:10.1038/s41598-019-53023-6; Plant J. 2019 doi:10.1111/tpj.14516; Plant Physiol. 2019 (Zager et al.)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper notes UGT90A1 family membership had not previously been reported to possess OA glycosylation activity.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Glycosylation of pathway intermediates OA and DA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-Glc to olivetolic acid and divarinic acid, producing predominantly OAG1 and DAG1 isomers
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Glycosylated olivetolic acid and glycosylated divarinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: glycosylated cannabinoid intermediates; phenolic acid derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Female flowers and leaves for OA/DA glycosides in examined cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Enhances water solubility; may alter cellular localization and regulate intermediate availability
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the natural major OA and DA glycoside isomers found in planta and shows narrower substrate specificity focused on pathway intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Candidate mining; qRT-PCR; heterologous expression in Escherichia coli; purified enzyme assays with multiple substrates; LC-MS isomer profiling; steady-state kinetics
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly elevated in flowers across flowering stages compared with leaves; pattern mirrors OAC and THCAS
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Potential regulatory checkpoint but not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsUGT11 glycosylates OA and DA in vitro. 2. It is the only identified Cannabis UGT that predominantly forms OAG1, the common natural OA-glycoside isomer in the plant. 3. It has narrower substrate scope than CsUGT14, acting mainly on pathway intermediates. 4. Its flower-enriched expression matches cannabinoid biosynthetic genes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Intermediate glycosylation may divert flux away from downstream cannabinoids
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate gene to manipulate OA/DA sequestration or flux control in cannabinoid biosynthesis; caution because boosting activity may reduce end-product yield
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Differential expression analysis using three published transcriptome datasets: Sci. Rep. 2019 doi:10.1038/s41598-019-53023-6; Plant J. 2019 doi:10.1111/tpj.14516; Plant Physiol. 2019 (Zager et al.)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: None of the tested Cannabis UGTs produced OAG3 in vitro, so CsUGT11 explains OAG1 but not OAG3 accumulation in planta.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Glycosylation of OA/DA intermediates and multiple cannabinoid end products
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-Glc to OA, DA, CBGA, CBDA, THCA, CBCA, CBNA, THCVA, CBG, and CBD; wild type predominantly forms OAG2 and broad cannabinoid glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Olivetolic acid; divarinic acid; CBGA; CBDA; THCA; CBCA; CBNA; THCVA; CBG; CBD; UDP-Glc
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: OAG2; DAG3; CBGA-G1; CBGA-G2; CBDA-G1; glycosylated THCA/CBCA/CBNA/THCVA/CBG/CBD products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Female flowers and leaves for several glycosides detected in planta; enzyme additionally forms glycosides in vitro not confidently detected in planta
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improves water solubility and may enhance pharmaceutical or cosmetic utility; OA glycosylation may modulate compartmentation and pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major broad-specificity cannabinoid UGT; active on OA and numerous cannabinoid substrates, and engineering of this enzyme increased specificity toward cannabinoid end products over OA intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic and transcriptome mining; qRT-PCR; heterologous expression in Escherichia coli; purified enzyme assays; LC-MS isomer analysis; steady-state kinetics; AlphaFold2/3 modeling; FuncLib active-site design; PROSS stabilization; docking-guided variant characterization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly elevated in flowers across flowering stages compared with leaves; similar to OAC and THCAS
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developmental stage during flowering; in vitro enzyme engineering context
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Acts as a key controllable branch-point enzyme for glycosylation versus cannabinoid flux, but not established as native rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsUGT14 was the most active OA-glycosylating UGT identified from Cannabis. 2. It shows broad substrate promiscuity, glycosylating 10 of 12 tested cannabinoid-related substrates. 3. Wild-type CsUGT14 predominantly forms the OAG2 isomer, unlike the major natural OAG1 in planta. 4. A single Glu86Gln change shifts OA positioning and switches isomer output toward OAG1. 5. PROSS stabilization increased expression and thermal stability, and FuncLib active-site variants increased glycosylation preference for cannabinoid end products over OA. 6. Second-round designs further improved CBCA and THCA glycosylation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Native OA glycosylation diverts flux away from cannabinoid production; engineered variants may alter isomer distributions and substrate promiscuity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value biocatalyst for tailored synthesis of water-soluble glycosylated cannabinoids in heterologous systems; engineer stabilized and active-site-modified versions to reduce OA diversion and favor end-product glycosylation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Differential expression analysis using three published transcriptome datasets: Sci. Rep. 2019 doi:10.1038/s41598-019-53023-6; Plant J. 2019 doi:10.1111/tpj.14516; Plant Physiol. 2019 (Zager et al.)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CsUGT14_stbl accession PX270907 and codon-optimized CsUGT14_opt accession PX270906 were characterized as engineered derivatives. Some variants produced OAG3 and a previously undetected THCA-G1 isomer. Different isomerization patterns occurred on wild-type versus stabilized backgrounds, likely due to altered dynamics.
